name: Kafka Topic Data Transmitted Without Encryption
description: Kafka cluster configured without TLS on broker-to-client and broker-to-broker connections transmits messages in plaintext, allowing network-level attackers or compromised internal hosts to intercept sensitive event data in transit.
mitigations: Enable TLS on all Kafka listeners (ssl.endpoint.identification.algorithm=https)
Configure inter-broker encryption with SSL keystores
Use client certificate authentication alongside TLS to prevent unauthorized connections
Rotate TLS certificates annually with automated tooling

name: Kafka Missing Topic-Level ACLs Allowing Unauthorized Reads
description: Kafka cluster with no topic ACLs or with PLAINTEXT JAAS configuration allows any consumer group to subscribe to any topic, enabling unauthorized services to read sensitive event streams (PII, financial transactions, audit logs).
mitigations: Enable Kafka's ACL authorization (authorizer.class.name=kafka.security.auth.SimpleAclAuthorizer)
Define per-topic ACLs with minimum-necessary consumer groups
Use SASL/SCRAM or mTLS for client authentication before applying ACLs
Audit Kafka ACLs quarterly and remove stale consumer permissions

name: RabbitMQ Default Credentials on Management Interface
description: RabbitMQ instance deployed with default credentials (guest/guest) on the management interface allows unauthenticated attackers to gain full administrative access, publish malicious messages, consume queues, and reconfigure the broker.
mitigations: Disable the guest account or change its password immediately on deployment
Restrict management interface access to internal network or VPN only
Use dedicated application credentials with minimum-necessary virtual host permissions
Enable TLS on the management interface and AMQP ports

name: RabbitMQ Vhost Misconfiguration Enabling Cross-Tenant Message Access
description: Multi-tenant application using a single RabbitMQ vhost for all tenants with insufficiently scoped exchange/queue permissions allows one tenant's consumer to bind to another tenant's queue and read their messages.
mitigations: Use separate RabbitMQ virtual hosts per tenant for strong isolation
Apply per-queue write and read permissions using RabbitMQ's user permission model
Prefix queue and exchange names with tenant identifiers to make confusion visible
Audit RabbitMQ permissions with rabbitmqctl list_permissions on each vhost

name: Redis No Authentication Allowing Unauthenticated Access
description: Redis instance running without the requirepass configuration or without ACL authentication allows any host with network connectivity to read, write, or delete all keys, including session tokens, cached credentials, and application state.
mitigations: Set requirepass or use Redis ACL (Redis 6+) with a strong password on all instances
Bind Redis to localhost or a private interface — never expose to public internet
Use Redis TLS mode (tls-port) for in-transit protection
Enable Redis protected-mode to reject external connections without credentials

name: Redis Stream Consumer Group Manipulation
description: Attacker with write access to Redis (via a compromised service or misconfigured ACL) manipulates consumer group offsets using XGROUP SETID, causing legitimate consumers to skip processing messages or reprocess already-handled events.
mitigations: Apply Redis ACL rules to restrict XGROUP SETID and XDEL to privileged admin accounts only
Monitor consumer group lag with Redis XINFO GROUPS and alert on unexpected resets
Implement idempotent message handlers that safely handle duplicate delivery
Log all Redis admin commands using Redis MONITOR in audit mode

name: Kafka Consumer Group Offset Manipulation
description: Attacker with Kafka admin privileges using kafka-consumer-groups.sh to reset offsets causes consumers to reprocess historical events or skip future events, disrupting data pipelines, billing systems, or audit trails.
mitigations: Restrict KAFKA_ADMIN_ACTIONS permissions to dedicated operations accounts via ACLs
Require peer approval and audit logging for all offset reset operations
Alert on consumer group offset resets via Kafka metrics or a monitoring sidecar
Implement idempotent event processing to tolerate occasional redelivery

name: RabbitMQ Queue Flooding by Unthrottled Publisher
description: Publisher application without rate limiting or backpressure handling floods RabbitMQ queues with millions of messages, exhausting broker memory and disk, causing the broker to block connections and drop messages from all producers.
mitigations: Configure RabbitMQ memory and disk watermarks to activate publisher backpressure
Set per-queue message count or byte limits using queue arguments
Implement application-level rate limiting on publishers before enqueueing
Use lazy queues to page messages to disk and reduce broker memory pressure

name: Redis Keyspace Notification Leaking Sensitive Event Data
description: Redis keyspace notifications enabled without ACL controls allow any subscriber to listen for key operations on sensitive namespaces (session:*, secret:*), inferring user activity patterns and potentially capturing key names containing sensitive identifiers.
mitigations: Disable Redis keyspace notifications unless explicitly required (notify-keyspace-events '')
Use ACL rules to restrict SUBSCRIBE and PSUBSCRIBE to authorized services only
Avoid encoding sensitive data in key names — use opaque identifiers
Monitor keyspace notification subscriber count and alert on unexpected subscribers

name: Kafka Schema Registry Unauthenticated Access
description: Confluent Schema Registry deployed without authentication allows any client to register malformed or malicious schemas, causing producers to serialize incompatible messages and consumers to crash on deserialization, disrupting data pipelines.
mitigations: Enable Schema Registry authentication with HTTP Basic or OAuth2
Apply schema compatibility rules (FORWARD, BACKWARD, FULL) to prevent breaking changes
Restrict schema registration to CI/CD pipelines with dedicated service accounts
Monitor Schema Registry audit logs for unexpected schema registrations

name: RabbitMQ Shovel Plugin SSRF
description: Attacker with RabbitMQ management API access configures the Shovel plugin to forward messages to an attacker-controlled AMQP endpoint, exfiltrating message content, or uses the shovel to probe internal AMQP services via SSRF.
mitigations: Restrict shovel configuration to admin-only operations and require MFA for admin access
Use RabbitMQ's network partitioning settings to limit outbound connections from the broker
Audit shovel definitions via rabbitmqctl list_parameters and alert on new additions
Disable the Shovel plugin if dynamic message forwarding is not a business requirement

name: Redis Persistence Without Encryption at Rest
description: Redis instance with RDB or AOF persistence enabled without filesystem encryption stores snapshots and append-only logs in plaintext on disk, exposing all cached data (session tokens, API keys, PII) if the disk or backup is compromised.
mitigations: Enable full-disk encryption (LUKS, BitLocker) on Redis data volumes
Encrypt Redis RDB/AOF files at rest using a dedicated encryption layer if full-disk encryption is unavailable
Store Redis snapshots in encrypted S3 buckets or GCS with server-side encryption
Treat Redis backups with the same sensitivity as database backups
